serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of raw pork liver is approximately 85 g, equivalent to a palm-sized slice before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, variety meats and by-products, liver, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168375); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database (cr_id F06090030)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central; Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol are taken from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for pork liver, raw (cr_id F06090030), which is an approved primary regional source. These values are confirmed consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 168375 ('Pork, fresh, variety meats and by-products, liver, raw'), which is the approved secondary global source used for saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, sugar, and extended micronutrient values (vitamins, additional minerals, water content) not reported by HPB. Both sources are on the approved source list.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — pork liver, raw contains negligible available carbohydrates (2.47 g/100 g as glycogen); no GI value assigned. Reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pork liver, raw is a high-protein organ meat with only 2.47 g carbohydrate per 100 g, present primarily as glycogen rather than available starch or sugar. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign a GI value to organ meats or high-protein foods with negligible available carbohydrate. Per established GI methodology (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care), foods with insufficient carbohydrate content to elicit a measurable glycaemic response are classified as not applicable. Glycemic index is therefore set to null and gi_category to not_applicable. Glycemic load per serving is set to 0.0 as a conventional placeholder consistent with negligible available carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (87 mg/100 g) with favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio (potassium 273 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Raw pork liver has a low sodium content of 87 mg per 100 g and a potassium content of 273 mg per 100 g, yielding a favourable Na:K ratio that supports blood pressure regulation; however, individuals with combined hypertension and dyslipidaemia should limit consumption due to the high cholesterol content of 301 mg per 100 g, and must avoid high-sodium cooking sauces such as soy sauce or oyster sauce commonly used in liver preparations.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.47 g/100 g), high protein (21.39 g/100 g), negligible glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pork liver is a very low-carbohydrate, high-protein food with negligible glycaemic impact, making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; the exceptional iron (23.3 mg/100 g), vitamin B12 (23.4 mcg/100 g), and folate (163 mcg DFE/100 g) content supports metabolic function, but the very high cholesterol content (301 mg/100 g) warrants limiting consumption to occasional servings and avoiding sugary marinades or sweet sauces during preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high dietary cholesterol (301 mg/100 g) and moderate saturated fat (1.23 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pork liver contains 301 mg of cholesterol per 100 g, which is exceptionally high and represents a significant concern for individuals with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia; consumption should be limited to small, infrequent portions, and preparation methods that add saturated fat (e.g., deep-frying in lard) should be avoided, while the high iron and B-vitamin content may offer some cardiovascular benefit when consumed in moderation as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High arachidonic acid (pro-inflammatory omega-6) and high purine content in organ meat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pork liver is an organ meat rich in arachidonic acid, a pro-inflammatory omega-6 fatty acid that amplifies cytokine production, and is high in purines; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit consumption to occasional small servings and pair with anti-inflammatory vegetables and spices to partially offset the inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork liver, raw, is the uncooked liver of the domestic pig (Sus scrofa domesticus), classified as an organ meat within the meat and alternatives food group. It presents as a dark reddish-brown organ with a smooth, dense, and slightly firm texture when raw, transitioning to a firmer, grainy texture upon cooking. Raw pork liver has a strong, iron-rich, slightly metallic aroma characteristic of organ meats. It is consumed widely across East Asian cuisines (Chinese stir-fries, congee toppings), Southeast Asian preparations (Filipino sinanglaw, Vietnamese chao gan), and European traditions (pâté, liverwurst). Nutritionally, pork liver is one of the most nutrient-dense foods available: it provides 134 kcal, 21.39 g protein, 3.65 g fat, and only 2.47 g carbohydrate per 100 g. It is an exceptional source of iron (23.3 mg/100 g — approximately 130% of the adult daily reference intake), vitamin B12 (23.4 mcg/100 g), folate (163 mcg DFE/100 g), vitamin A (6502 mcg RAE/100 g), riboflavin (2.61 mg/100 g), and selenium (35.5 mcg/100 g). The very high cholesterol content (301 mg/100 g) is a key clinical consideration for individuals with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia. The glycaemic index is not applicable given the negligible available carbohydrate content. Pork liver is not halal and not kosher. It is gluten-free and dairy-free. It is suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns in moderation, and is particularly valuable for addressing iron-deficiency anaemia and B12 deficiency when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06090030, confidence 0.95), which is an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat, fatty acid fractions, sugar, and extended micronutrient values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork liver, raw (FDC ID 168375), which is an approved secondary global source. Both sources are in close agreement on core macros. Confidence is rated high.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 168375 — organ meats contain no dietary fibre. 2. sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168375) — the carbohydrate present is glycogen, not free sugar. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw pork liver. 4. Glycemic index is set to null and gi_category to 'not_applicable': pork liver is a high-protein organ meat with only 2.47 g carbohydrate per 100 g (primarily glycogen); glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible available carbohydrates. gi_source references the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) as the methodological basis for this classification. gi_source.source_match_type is set to 'analogous_estimate' per template requirements. 5. Saturated fat (1.23 g), monounsaturated fat (0.56 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.54 g), and extended micronutrient values (vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, minerals, water) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 168375 as HPB does not report these fields; values are consistent with the HPB-reported macros. 6. is_halal is set to false: pork is not permissible under Islamic dietary law. is_kosher is set to false: pork is not permissible under Jewish dietary law. 7. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03311349 and FoodEx2 code A0FKP are used as defensible anchors; SNOMED CT code 226934003 is cited as a defensible match. Reviewers should verify these codes against current ontology releases. 8. The input record listed gi_value as 0 with confidence 0.4 from 'Embedded GI Database' — this has been corrected to null/not_applicable per Veritas q4 rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods. 9. nutrient_source.source_match_type is set to 'exact' reflecting that USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 168375 is an exact match for pork liver raw, and HPB cr_id F06090030 is also an exact match; the combination of two approved exact-match sources is documented in source_match_notes.

All three mandatory health conditions present with specific clinical guidance. Type 2 Diabetes human_condition_name correctly set. Multilingual coverage provided in Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi. Ontology anchors are defensible with exact match types.
